input_path: data\papers\Mertens and Denil - 2025 - Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: Lab‑scale gantry crane comprising a physical gantry structure, load carriage, and an attached controller; operates in a laboratory setting for demonstration and research. The system is small‑scale, open‑source, and highly modular, featuring explicit kinematic relationships and readily measurable states—characteristics that make it suitable for digital twinning.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Provides services for optimal control of crane motion, continuous validation against the kinematic model, historical data logging for analysis, and real‑time data visualization via dashboards. These services support researchers, educators, and developers, enabling monitoring, optimization, prediction, control assistance, and visual analysis of system behavior.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Relies on freely available, commonly used software: CAD models supply the geometrical representation; a kinematic model defines motion relationships; no specific commercial tools are cited, but the twin uses standard open‑source simulation and data handling tools to maintain its services.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The lab‑scale gantry crane digital twin employs a containerised micro‑service architecture orchestrated with Docker Compose. Each functional fragment – data acquisition from the PLC, message translation, a time‑series database layer, simulation/modeling, and visualization – runs in its own Docker container. Inter‑service communication is performed over an MQTT broker (Eclipse Mosquitto) using QoS 1 to relay real‑time sensor readings and actuator commands between the physical crane and the digital twin. Raw telemetry streams are persisted in a TimescaleDB instance which exposes a PostgreSQL‑compatible interface; the simulation module queries this database to compute predictive states. A Grafana instance, also containerised, consumes TimescaleDB data via the SQL plugin and renders real‑time dashboards. Docker Compose not only allocates network ports and volumes but also manages service discovery, automatic restarts, and scalable deployment, thereby providing a lightweight orchestration layer for the entire twin ecosystem.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external systems is implemented through the low‑cost greenhouse’s Raspberry‑Pi sensor nodes that publish telemetry to the ActiveMQ broker. The twin stores incoming data in InfluxDB and exposes it to downstream processing components running on virtual machines. Microsoft Azure tooling is employed for cloud‑based simulation and prediction pipelines, leveraging Azure Functions or Azure IoT Hub as described. Code and configuration are versioned and distributed via GitHub, providing a release mechanism for the entire twin stack. No explicit REST APIs, OPC UA, or enterprise broker integration is described in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The digital twin constellation relies on a solid CAD representation of the gantry crane’s structural geometry and an analytical kinematic model that predicts the position of the hoist and carriage during motions.  Time‑series data from the operating plant are stored in a TimescaleDB instance, which is exploited for real‑time monitoring and for historical trajectory replay.  The CAD and kinematic models are tightly coupled through the twin’s simulation engine, enabling a closed‑loop comparison between measured actuation states and the model predictions.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation is driven by industrial stepper motors configured via a Python control program that issues step commands to the motor drivers; the system also incorporates an electromagnet for optional holding force. The stepper motors provide precise positional increments without continuous power consumption. Communication with the motors is handled over a local network interface, with the Python program timestamping actuation events.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing suite includes a swing‑angle encoder mounted on the gantry to monitor the rotational position of the platform, and an anemometer installed on the crane arm to measure wind speed acting on the platform. Both sensors feed data into the Twins’ database in near real‑time; the encoder’s output is used by the validation service, while the anemometer data is available for runtime visualisation and predictive modelling.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is ensured by the Continuous Validation Service, which compares each executed motion trajectory to the kinematic simulation, flagging deviations beyond an acceptable threshold. Validation employs historical trajectory replay and continuous monitoring, with error detection prompting re‑simulation and verification through cross‑checking logged sensor data against simulation outputs.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data about the gantry crane's state is collected by the Python-based Physical Crane Interface and continuously forwarded by the Logging Application to a TimescaleDB database, which serves as the data store for the digital twin. The sensor data includes positional and velocity information of the crane's actuators, as well as any other measured attributes captured by the physical hardware. No explicit transmission protocol or frequency is specified beyond the description of 'continuous' logging, and the document does not detail any data preprocessing or quality-assurance steps. Integration into the digital twin occurs by persisting the raw measurements in TimescaleDB, which can then be visualized by the Grafana dashboard.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands originate from the Control Application, a Python program that uses the Physical Crane Interface to send instructions such as 'move to position X' or 'execute trajectory Y' to the actual crane hardware. The Physical Crane Interface communicates these commands to the crane controllers, though the specific message format, authentication, or validation mechanisms are not described in the document. Feedback from the physical system—such as acknowledgments of command execution or updated state—is not explicitly detailed. Conflict resolution and command execution confirmation mechanisms are similarly not specified in the provided material.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The only explicitly named communication backbone is an MQTT broker that services inter‑service signaling (e.g., between Simulation Service, Continuous Validation Service, etc.). The document does not describe the network topology, underlying protocols for other inter‑container connections, bandwidth, latency, redundancy, or security features – only that the broker is used for signaling. No explicit mention of HTTP, gRPC, or TCP sockets is provided, nor any details on security protocols such as TLS/SSL, authentication, or firewall configuration.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: All software components run on a single personal computer (edge/on‑premise). Each component – Physical Crane Interface, Control Application, Logging Application, Dashboard, Trajectory Generator Service, Simulation Service, Continuous Validation Service, MQTT Broker, TimescaleDB, and Grafana – is containerized using Docker and orchestrated via a Docker‑Compose file. Computational load is moderate, as the containers are lightweight Python services, with TimescaleDB requiring storage for time‑series data. The deployment model is single‑node, on‑prem, with no explicit scaling or high‑availability architecture described beyond the containerization approach.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil

block_1_judge.reasoning: Accurately describes the lab-scale gantry crane, including its physical structure (gantry, load carriage, controller), lab setting, small-scale and open-source nature, and suitability for digital twinning due to kinematic relationships and measurable states. Supported by quotes: 'lab-scale gantry crane', 'physical crane and its controller', 'kinematics model of the crane', 'freely available and commonly used software'. Lacks some specific component details (e.g., motors, electromagnet) present in the text.

Correctly lists services such as optimal control, continuous validation, historical data logging, and real-time visualization via dashboards. Mentions target users (researchers, educators, developers) and service capabilities. Supported by quotes: 'services for optimal control, historical data logging, data visualization and continuous validation', 'Trajectory Generator Service', 'Continuous Validation Service', 'Dashboard'. Could include more technical depth (e.g., specific validation methods) for a higher score.

Correctly notes reliance on CAD models, kinematic models, and open-source tools. Supported by quotes: 'CAD models of the crane made in Autodesk Fusion360', 'kinematics model of the crane', 'freely available and commonly used software', 'TimescaleDB', 'Grafana', 'Eclipse Mosquitto', 'Docker'. The description is accurate but somewhat generic; it could list specific tools to be more detailed.

_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

block_2_judge.reasoning: The characteristic correctly states 'Not in Document'. The source text mentions 'continuous logging' and 'real-time status', but does not provide specific details regarding synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, or temporal granularity needs as defined in the task instructions.

The characteristic correctly states 'Not in Document'. The source text focuses on a single lab-scale gantry crane system. While it briefly mentions an expansion to a 'ship digital twin' in a teaching context, it does not describe specific multiplicities, hierarchical structures, or coordination mechanisms between twin instances within the detailed system architecture.

The extracted description accurately reflects the architecture described in the source documents. It correctly identifies the use of Docker Compose for orchestration and lists the specific containers: Database (TimescaleDB), Dashboard (Grafana), MQTT Broker (Eclipse Mosquitto), and the various services. The description correctly states that inter-service communication happens via MQTT and that the database uses a PostgreSQL-compatible interface. The mention of service discovery and automatic restarts via Docker Compose is also supported by the context of containerized orchestration. Specific quotes supporting this include: 'The digital twin consists of six containers... Database... Dashboard... MQTT Broker...', 'For ease of deployment, the Database, MQTT Broker and Dashboard C4 Containers are containerized with Docker. A docker compose file is provided...', and 'For the database, the choice for TimescaleDB was driven by three factors: (i) it’s based on PostgreSQL...'

The extraction incorrectly attributes characteristics of the 'GreenhouseDT' and 'Air Quality Management' exemplars (discussed in the Background section) to the main Lab-Scale Gantry Crane system. It claims integration via 'low-cost greenhouse’s Raspberry-Pi sensor nodes' and 'InfluxDB', whereas the Gantry Crane uses 'TimescaleDB', 'Eclipse Mosquitto', and a local PC architecture. This makes the description irrelevant to the specific system under study.

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the digital twin components present in the source. It correctly identifies the 'CAD models' and 'kinematic model' mentioned in the abstract and the 'TimescaleDB database' used for logging (Section III.A). The description of the 'closed-loop comparison' aligns with the 'Continuous Validation Service' which 'compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations' (Section III.A). While 'historical trajectory replay' is a slight inference from 'historical data logging' and 'visualization', the overall technical details are well-supported.

While the extraction correctly identifies stepper motors and a Python control interface, it adds significant unsupported technical details. The text does not mention 'step commands,' stepper behavior regarding power consumption ('without continuous power consumption'), or network-based communication ('local network interface'). These appear to be outside knowledge or speculation.

The extraction correctly identifies the swing-angle encoder and anemometer, as well as the visualization dashboard. However, it inaccurately describes the encoder as monitoring the 'platform' (text says 'hoist') and adds an unsupported claim that anemometer data is used for 'predictive modelling' (text only mentions visualization and logging).

The extraction provides a good summary of the Continuous Validation Service, correctly mentioning trajectory comparison, distance metrics, and thresholds. However, it incorrectly states that error detection prompts 're-simulation' and relies on 'historical trajectory replay' for validation; the text states that operators are notified to take action, rather than an automatic re-simulation loop occurring.

block_4_docs_retrieved:
III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the data flow from the Physical Crane Interface and Logging Application to the TimescaleDB database, correctly noting that logging is 'continuous'. It accurately points out the lack of explicit transmission protocols and frequencies. The mention of 'positional and velocity information' is supported by references to measurements of 'executed trajectories' and kinematic models.

The extraction correctly identifies the Control Application and Physical Crane Interface as the origin and medium for control commands. It accurately infers command types (e.g., 'move to position') based on the descriptions of the Trajectory Generator and Control Application. It is also correct in stating that specific message formats and validation mechanisms are not detailed in the text.

Accurately identifies the 'MQTT Broker' as the communication backbone used for 'signaling between services', directly supported by the source. Correctly points out the lack of detail regarding network topology, other protocols (HTTP, gRPC), bandwidth, latency, or security features, stating 'The document does not describe...'. The extraction is precise, well-grounded, and perfectly captures the scope of technical connection details provided.

Correctly identifies that all software runs on a 'single personal computer' and that the 'Database, MQTT Broker and Dashboard... are containerized with Docker'. It lists the components accurately. The claim about 'Computational load is moderate' and 'lightweight Python services' is an interpretation not explicitly in the text, which mentions the computers are 'personal' but doesn't specify load. Nevertheless, the description of the deployment model (single-node, on-prem, Docker Compose) is excellent and specific.

block_5_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_
